nativeRange.habitat: Clear-water lagoon patch reefs and seaward reefs; typically found from the lower surge zone to approximately 45 m depth. Has been observed sheltering among sea grass, long-spined urchins, and rocky crevices during the day, emerging at night to forage.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty frozen foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped marine flesh. In the wild, tends to prey on small crustaceans and mobile invertebrates. Has been known to prefer feeding after the aquarium lights dim, consistent with its nocturnal tendencies; offering food in the evening tends to improve acceptance for newly acquired specimens.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3358x2519. Full side profile showing diagnostic iridescent green-blue scale sheen, spotted dorsal fins and black caudal-peduncle spot; sharp and well-lit.
Philippe Bourjon; 1200x866. Sharp reddish-bronze adult in a reef crevice; black caudal-peduncle spot and striped caudal fin clearly visible.
Rickard Zerpe; 2379x1784. Sharp shot with gold head, white-edged eye stripe, dark midlateral stripe and clear black caudal spot; adds angle variety.

summary: A slow-moving, peaceful Indo-Pacific cardinalfish generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe. Tends to be nocturnal; has been reported to hide in caves and urchin spines by day, foraging for small crustaceans after dark.
careNotes:
The Band Tail Black Spot Cardinalfish (Pristiapogon kallopterus) is also sold under the name Iridescent Cardinalfish — an iridescent green-to-blue sheen visible under certain lighting is a characteristic field mark. The older genus name Apogon kallopterus remains common in the aquarium trade, though current taxonomy places this species in genus Pristiapogon.

This species tends to be a slow, methodical swimmer that generally does not compete aggressively for territory or food. In the aquarium it has been known to shelter among rockwork, artificial caves, or the spines of long-spined urchins during daylight, becoming more active after the lights dim. Offering food after lights-out is generally advised for new arrivals that appear reluctant to eat.

When kept in small groups, this species tends to establish a social hierarchy without significant aggression; groups of three or more are sometimes kept successfully in larger aquariums. It is generally considered reef-safe, though its carnivorous diet means ornamental shrimp and very small crustaceans may occasionally be at risk.

Aquarists in regions with warmer ambient temperatures may find specimens do well toward the upper end of this range.

Lifespan data in captivity have not been documented in available sources.

Like many Apogonidae, this species is believed to be a paternal mouthbrooder in the wild, though captive breeding reports for this particular species are limited in available hobby sources.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely documented in available hobby sources. As a member of family Apogonidae, this species is likely a paternal mouthbrooder (the male carries the egg mass in his mouth until hatching), consistent with the family norm. A swollen jaw and reduced feeding in a male after spawning tend to be indicators of active brooding.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Apogon kallopterus confirmed as synonym), adult size (6 in / 15.24 cm), native range (Tahiti, Indo-Pacific, Red Sea), and diet (frozen meaty foods; notes nocturnal feeding tendency). Limited parameter data available on this page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Apogonidae, order Kurtiformes), current valid scientific name Pristiapogon kallopterus (Bleeker, 1856) — confirmed genus change from Apogon. Confirmed maximum size 15.5 cm TL, preferred temperature 23.9–28.9°C (based on 1254 occurrence data points), distribution across Indo-Pacific from Red Sea to Hawaii and New Zealand, and benthopelagic/nocturnal reef-associated habitat. Temperature range differs from LiveAquaria (72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C); averaged to 23.0–27.3°C for final values.

fish.breedingNotes: Not documented in captivity in available sources. Suspected paternal mouthbrooder consistent with family Apogonidae.
